segments.s490-g1: سکندر (subject) + چو + past verb form بَشنید creates a temporal clause: چو here = 'when'. بَشنید is 3rd pers. sing. past 'heard'. از introduces the topic/source ('about' or 'concerning') and links to یادگیر, a noun meaning 'the learner' (the person he heard about). The clause therefore means 'When Alexander heard about the learner...'.
segments.s490-g2: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said'. تا here introduces the result/purpose clause ('that/...'), not temporal 'until'. پیش او = 'in his presence' (پیش + pronoun او). شد is the past of شدن 'became/was made' and takes دبیر (a predicate noun) to mean 'was made/appointed a scribe' — the subject of شد is understood from context (someone was brought/made into the scribe's presence).
segments.s490-g3: نوشتند is 3rd pers. pl. past 'they wrote'. پس = 'then/after' modifies the temporal sequence. نامه‌ای is the indefinite object 'a letter' (ـی marks indefiniteness). بر حریر is a prepositional phrase 'on silk' (بر + noun), placed after the object as is common in Persian poetic order.
segments.s490-g4: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the origin/author of the letter. شیراوژن is a proper name, followed by apposition اسکندر (Alexander) and the epithet شهرگیر 'city-conqueror'—all together indicate the sender/attribution: 'from Shirāwuzan, Alexander the city-conqueror.'
segments.s490-g5: به نزدیک is an adverbial phrase 'in the vicinity / in the presence of' (به + نزدیک). قیدافه is a proper name; هوشمند follows it as an adjectival epithet 'the wise/clever'. There is no copula; this is a nominal phrase meaning 'near Qeydāfeh, the wise.'
segments.s490-g6: شده is the past participle/past form of شدن 'became' used here as main verb 'became/rose'. نام او is the possessed noun 'his name' (نام + pronoun او). در بزرگی 'in greatness' is a locative/figurative phrase introduced by در, and بلند 'high/lofty' modifies the foregoing phrase: 'his name became lofty in greatness.'
segments.s490-g7: نخست 'first' sets the ordering. آفرین here is a noun 'praise/acclamation' (not the verb stem). خداوند is 'the Lord' or 'God' and مهر functions as an epithet or name (here treated as the deity/title 'Mehr')—the whole phrase is an opening formula giving praise.
segments.s490-g8: فروزنده is a present-participial adjective 'illuminating/brightening' which governs ماه 'the moon' (فروزنده ماه = 'who lights the moon'). و links the next participial epithet; گردان is a participle/adjective 'revolving/turning' applied to سپهر 'the sky/heavens' (گردان سپهر = 'the one who turns the heavens'). Both epithets describe the preceding divine name.
segments.s490-g17: خردمندی و پیش‌بینی کنی is a nominal phrase (خردمندی “wisdom” + conjunction و + پیش‌بینی “foresight”) followed by کنی, the present-subjunctive/imperative form of کردن used here as an exhortation: “do/possess.” The word order places the abstract qualities before the verb کنی, so the clause reads literally “(do) wisdom and foresight,” i.e. “show/be wise and farsighted.”
segments.s490-g18: توانایی و پاک دینی کنی mirrors the previous structure: two nouns/adjectival phrases (توانایی “ability”; پاک دینی “pure religion/piety”) joined by و and scoped by the same verb کنی. پاک (adjective) modifies دینی, and کنی again functions as an exhortative verb, so the phrase means “(show) ability and pure piety.”
segments.s490-g19: وگر هیچ تاب اندر آری به کار is a conditional clause: وگر (if) + هیچ تاب (no endurance) + اندر (into/in) + آری. Here تاب means “endurance.” آری appears in a poetically archaic form (context suggests a verb like “bring/put/have”), which is not represented among the provided noun senses, so its verbal force is noted rather than matched to the given dictionary senses. به کار (“to the work/task”) marks the domain where that endurance would be applied.
segments.s490-g20: نبینی جز از گردش روزگار is the result clause: نبینی is the negative present/subjunctive of دیدن used here to state what one will (not) see; جز introduces the exception “except”; از گردش روزگار is a genitive-like phrase meaning “from the turning/rotation of the times,” i.e. the vicissitudes of fate. The verb is linked back to the conditional: if you lack endurance, you will see nothing but the turns of fortune.
segments.s490-g21: چو اندازه‌گیری ز دارا و فور begins with چو “when,” introducing a temporal/conditional clause. اندازه‌گیری is a verbal noun (measuring/judging); ز marks the standard or source (“by/from”), so ز دارا و فور means “by the wealthy and by suddenness/abruptness” (i.e., judging by riches and immediate appearances). The phrase warns about taking such criteria as your measure.
segments.s490-g22: خود آموزگارت نباید ز دور is the main clause that follows: خود آموزگارت “your own teacher” (خود = own) is the subject, and نباید is an impersonal modal “should not/ought not” indicating advisability; ز دور literally “from far” means “far away.” Together: “you should not keep your own teacher at a distance.”
segments.s490-g23: چو از باد عنوان او گشت خشک is another temporal clause: چو “when” + از باد “from/by the wind” (an ablative/agentive phrase) + عنوان او “his title/name/fame” + گشت (past of گشتن) “became” + خشک “dry/withering.” The structure describes a change (his title grew pale/faded) caused figuratively by the winds of rumor or time.
segments.s490-g24: نهادند مهری بروبر ز مشک is a past action: نهادند (they placed/set) governs a direct object مهری, here used as “a token/amulet/mark of affection” rather than a personal name; بروبر (“b-rūbar” in poetry) adverbially means “directly/exactly” or “face-to-face”; ز مشک specifies material or scent “of/from musk.” The clause says that they set/placed this affectionate token precisely of musk.
segments.s490-g9: This noun phrase names the subject and two abstract objects: خداوند (the Creator/God) + بخشنده (an epithet, 'the generous'). داد and راست are nouns in juxtaposition meaning 'justice' and 'truth' (classical Persian often lists paired abstract nouns). Word order here is Subject + Epithets + Nouns given as the things God has 'given' in a general, stative sense.
segments.s490-g10: This clause expresses what is fitting or due. فزونی کسی را دهد is literally 'gives increase to someone' (کسی را is the definite/indefinite object marked by را). The final سزاست ('it is deserved / fitting') comments on that action. The item کش here is archaic/poetic and does not match the senses supplied (it likely functions as a relative/particle meaning 'who/that' or is a poetic inflection); its precise role is inferential rather than a regular lexical meaning.
segments.s490-g11: به تندی is an adverbial phrase ('with speed / swiftly'), where به functions as an adverbial marker ('-ly/by') and تندی is 'haste/fierceness.' نجستیم is a past plural verb 'we leapt/sprung' (classical form of جستن). رزم ترا is a genitival/possessive phrase 'your battle' (ت = 2nd-person suffix; را here follows as an object/definite marker/emphatic). The clause means 'we sprang swiftly to/into your battle.'
segments.s490-g12: گراینده is a participial/noun form 'one who directs/causes to incline.' گشتیم is the past plural of شدن 'we became.' Together گراینده گشتیم = 'we became directors/turners/instigators.' بزم ترا is 'your assembly/banquet' with the 2nd-person suffix; the verb precedes the possessed noun, a common flexible order in classical Persian.
segments.s490-g13: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' این نامه آرند is a simple clause 'they bring this letter' (آرند = they bring/bring, classical present/subjunctive/indicative 3pl form of آوردن). نزدیک تو is an adverbial phrase 'near you.' The whole segment reads 'When they bring this letter near you...'.
segments.s490-g14: درخشان شود is a predication 'becomes shining/bright' (شود = becomes). رای تاریک تو is a noun phrase 'your dark counsel/opinion' (رای = plan/opinion/counsel, تاریک = dark/obscure, تو = your). Adjective follows the noun (typical order) and the verb (شود) takes the whole noun phrase as its complement: 'your dark counsel will become bright.'
segments.s490-g15: فرستی here is a 2nd-person imperative/optative form 'send' (addressing the addressee). به فرمان ما is a prepositional phrase 'by/at our command' (به used with sense 'by/‑ly' here). باژ و ساو are two proper names (or items to be sent); punctuation/coordination is by و. The whole segment is an instruction: 'Send, by our order, Bazh and Sāv.'
segments.s490-g16: بدانی is an imperative/subjunctive 'know (that)...' introducing the following clause with که ('that'). با ما ترا نیست تاو is structured 'with us (با ما) + ترا (you, object/accusative with suffix + را) + نیست (is not) + تاو (radiance/glow)' — i.e. 'know that your radiance is not with us.' The clause uses که to introduce content of 'know' and places the negation نیست before the predicate noun تاو.
segments.s490-g25: بیامد is a past simple verb meaning "came"; the verb precedes the subject in this poetic line. هیون تگاور is a compound name/epithet (a noun phrase), and به راه is a prepositional phrase meaning "along/on the road" (به + noun). The sequence Verb — (Name) — prepositional phrase is typical in Ferdowsi's elevated word order.
segments.s490-g26: به فرمان is an instrumental/agent phrase meaning "by the command (of)" where به is functioning as "by/through" in this context. آن نامبردار شاه is a demonstrative + adjective + noun phrase "that renowned/aforementioned king"; نامبردار is an epithet referring back to a previously mentioned sovereign.
segments.s490-g27: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning "when." قیدافه is a proper name (the addressee/reader), and آن نامه او is the noun phrase "that letter of his/that letter." بخواند is the past (or narrative) form of خواندن here meaning "he read/recited," so the whole clause is "when Qeydāfeh read that letter."
segments.s490-g28: ز گفتار او is an ablative/causal phrase "from/because of his words" (ز used poetically for "because of/at"). در شگفتی بماند is a predicative phrase: در + noun "in astonishment" + بماند (remain) — literally "remained in astonishment." The verb بماند is narrative/past here matching the earlier clause.
segments.s490-g29: به پاسخ نخست is an adverbial phrase "in answer, first" (به پاسخ = "as a reply", نخست = "first"). آفرین گسترید is a compact verb phrase: آفرین here functions as a noun/utterance "praise/acclamation," and گسترید (past/ narrative) "spread/uttered" — together meaning "uttered praise" or "gave a cry of praise."
segments.s490-g30: بدان is a dative/directional phrase formed from به + آن ("to that/at that"); here it means "to that (one)." دادگر کو ... گسترید is a relative clause describing that one: دادگر "the just/righteous (ruler)" + کو (classical relative/relative-like particle "who/which") + زمین گسترید "spread/made wide the earth" — so the whole segment = "to that just ruler who spread the earth."
segments.s490-g31: ترا کرد پیروز is a causative/perfect construction: ترا (ت + را = "you" with the object marker) is the direct object, کرد is the past 'made/did,' and پیروز is a predicative adjective "victorious" — "(he) made you victorious." بر + (فور) هند is a prepositional phrase giving the opponent/field: بر = "over/against," هند = "Hind/India," so the clause means "made you victorious over Hind (its forces)."
segments.s490-g32: به دارا و بر نامداران سند coordinates two prepositional phrases: به دارا likely means "over/against Dāra" or "as to Dāra" (به here marking beneficiary/target), و introduces the second, بر نامداران سند = "over the famed/renowned men of Sind" (بر = "over/against," نامداران = "renowned ones," سند = "Sind"). The structure adds further objects of victory.
segments.s490-g33: Word order is object (مرا + را) before the verb: مرا ... برابر نهی = “do not make/put me equal ...”. چو ایشان (“like them”) follows با and modifies برابر. نهی here is a negative imperative/forbidding addressed to ‘you’ (archaic/poetic form).
segments.s490-g34: به سر بر ز پیروزه افسر نهی is a compact locative phrase: به سر بر + (z) + [modifier] + افسر + نهی = “do not place (the) helmet of turquoise upon the head.” بر after سر marks ‘on/upon’, ز introduces the material/quality (from/of the turquoise), and نهی again is the negative imperative.
segments.s490-g35: This clause has a causal/contrastive connector زان (“because/for that”), with مرا + را marking the topic/affected person and فزونست (archaic fused verb: ‘is greater’) as the predicate: ‘for me greater is فر and مهی’. فر and مهی are the two nominal subjects that follow the predicate in poetic inversion.
segments.s490-g36: همان introduces the comparison/contrast (“even that / the very”) and is followed by two coordinated nouns—لشکر و گنج—qualified by شاهنشهی (“imperial/kingly”), completing the idea of what the speaker’s فر و مهی exceed.
segments.s490-g37: که introduces a subordinate clause expressing the feared result: من قیصران را به فرمان شوم = “that I may become subject to the Caesars’ command.” قیصران را is marked with را as the (dative/affected) object of becoming subject to فرمان; شوم is the optative/subjunctive/possibility form ‘may become’.
segments.s490-g38: This continues the feared consequences: بترسم ز تهدید = “I should fear because of threats.” بترسم is the present-subjunctive/optative of ترسیدن (‘to fear’). پیچان شوم is a poetic expression ‘become twisted/led astray’ (پیچان here = deceptive/twisting) with شوم again marking potential/result.
segments.s490-g39: هزاران هزارم is a poetic multiplicative expression (“thousands upon thousands”); the predicate is لشکرست (لشکر + است contracted), so the clause reads: ‘My innumerable host (is) more/abundant.’ The adjective فزون qualifies the abundance of the host.
segments.s490-g40: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause: بر هر سری شهریاری سرست = ‘for upon every head rests (a) sovereignty.’ بر هر سری = ‘upon each head’, شهریاری = ‘sovereignty/royal rule’, and سرست is a poetic predicative form meaning ‘is atop/serves as the head’ (i.e., rests/is placed).
segments.s490-g41: وگر is a conditional conjunction meaning “and if.” خوانم is first‑person singular present (classical/poetic) meaning “I call/summon” (here a habitual/conditional sense). The prepositional phrase از هر سوی (“from every direction/quarter”) follows the verb, and زیردست is the (collective) object “subordinate(s)/underlings” without overt plural marking.
segments.s490-g42: نماند is an impersonal/third‑person form meaning “there remains not / will not be left.” برین is the fused poetic form of بر + این (“on this”), so برین بوم = “on this land/earth.” جای نشست literally “place of sitting” = “place to sit/seat.” Word order places the negative verb before the locative phrase and nominal complement.
segments.s490-g43: یکی گنج is a simple nominal phrase “one/a single treasure.” در پیش is a locative idiom “before/in the presence of,” so در پیش هر مهتری means “before each chief/elder”; هر distributes the treasure to each individual chief.
segments.s490-g44: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” آید is third‑person singular present (classical usage for a future or habitual event) “he/it comes.” ازین is the fused form of از + این (“from this”), so ازین مرز = “from this border/frontier,” and با لشکری is an instrumental/associative phrase “with an army.”
segments.s490-g45: تو is the vocative/subject “you.” چندین چه is an emphatic interrogative cluster meaning roughly “how many times / how often” or “why so (many).” رانی is an archaic/poetic form here; context shows it behaves as a second‑person verbal form (addressing the addressee, “do you [do/say]”), but the provided dictionary entries do not capture this verbal use (see note). زبان بر گزاف is the object phrase “(your) tongue toward/into boastful/groundless speech” (گزاف = groundless/exaggerated).
segments.s490-g46: ز is the preposition “from/because of.” دارا in this context is the proper name Dārā (king/Darius) rather than the adjective “wealthy” (the provided senses omit the proper‑name reading). شدستی is an archaic second‑person perfect/past‑result form meaning “you became/you have become.” خداوند لاف is a nominal phrase “lord/master of boasting” (خداوند = lord/sovereign used as an honorific modifier of لاف ‘boast’).
segments.s490-g47: بران is a poetic deictic/adverb meaning “upon that / thereupon.” نامه‌بر is the agent noun “letter‑carrier / envoy.” مهر زرین is the noun phrase “golden seal” (مهر = seal/stamp) and نهاد is the simple past of نهادن “he placed/affixed.” The verb thus takes the messenger and the seal phrase as its complement.
segments.s490-g48: هیونی (هیون) is the poetic noun “beast/steed.” برافگند is a past tense verb meaning “he threw/sent off/scattered.” بر سان is a simile marker “like/as,” and باد here denotes the simile’s reference “the wind” (“sped like the wind”). The clause therefore describes sending the beast off with great speed.

sentences.s490-s1: When Alexander heard about the learner, he ordered that a scribe be brought before him.
sentences.s490-s2: They then wrote a letter on silk, from Shirāwuzan — Alexander the city-conqueror.
sentences.s490-s3: In the presence of the wise Qeydāfeh, his name rose high in greatness.
sentences.s490-s4: First, praise to the Lord — Mehr, the illuminator of the moon and the one who turns the heavens.
sentences.s490-s9: Be wise and foresighted; be capable and of pure faith.
sentences.s490-s10: And if you have no endurance for the task, you will see nothing but the turns of fate.
sentences.s490-s11: When you measure by wealth and by suddenness, you should not keep your own teacher far away.
sentences.s490-s12: When by the wind his title became dry, they placed a token of affection there, exactly of musk.
sentences.s490-s5: God the generous gave justice and truth; it is fitting that he give increase to whoever deserves it.
sentences.s490-s6: We leapt quickly into your battle; we became the directors (guiders) of your assembly.
sentences.s490-s7: When they bring this letter near you, your dark counsel will become bright.
sentences.s490-s8: Send, by our command, Bazh and Sāv; know that your radiance is not with us.
sentences.s490-s13: Hyun Tegāvār came along the road at the command of that famed king.
sentences.s490-s14: When Qeydāfeh read that letter, he was left astonished at what it said.
sentences.s490-s15: In answer he first uttered praise to that just one who had spread the earth.
sentences.s490-s16: He made you victorious over the forces of Hind, and over Dāra and the famed men of Sind.
sentences.s490-s17: Do not set me equal with them; do not place a turquoise helmet upon my head.
sentences.s490-s18: Because of that, my glory and moon‑like beauty are greater even than the imperial army and treasure.
sentences.s490-s19: Lest I come under the Caesars’ command; lest I fear their threats and be led astray.
sentences.s490-s20: My host is thousands upon thousands more; for upon every head there rests sovereignty.
sentences.s490-s21: And if I summon subordinates from every quarter, there will be no place left on this earth to sit.
sentences.s490-s22: A single treasure before each chief — when he comes from this frontier with an army.
sentences.s490-s23: You — why do you put your tongue to vain words so many times? Because of Dārā you have become a lord of boasting.
sentences.s490-s24: Thereupon he placed a golden seal on the messenger; he sent off the beast so that it sped like the wind.
